metadata.title: Bayesian Inverse Transition Learning for Offline Settings

figures.description: Data to Actionable Knowledge Lab Logo

sections.title: Summary
content: Learning a model is hard under low-coverage data
Having a model help be more data efficient
Inverse Transition Learning infers the dynamics using near-optimal demonstrations
We create a set of constraints to estimate in a gradient-free way an empirical posterior distribution over the transition dynamics T, where each sample has near-optimal guarantees
Show that planned policies are highly performant and exhibit lower variance across different batches of datas compared to MLE estimates
Explain how quantifying the uncertainty of the dynamics help planning more informative policies than the expert policy and the one obtained from MLE estimates

sections.title: What is Inverse Transition Learning?
content: Inverse Transition Learning

Infer the dynamics T given near-optimal demonstrations D, the reward function R and a near-optimal expert policy πe, where πeis assumed to only take actions that are ε-close to the best unknown action a*.

figures.description: Diagram showing the flow from Expert Behavior and Rewards through ITL to infer Dynamics

sections.title: Why is it hard?
content: Number of parameters grows with the dimensions of state and action spaces
Trade-off between a model that is expressive enough and one that is small enough to learn
Lack of coverage in offline inverse settings

sections.title: Why do we care?
content: Data efficiency
Simulation of new data
Off-Policy Evaluation
Counterfactual Reasoning

sections.title: Baselines
content: TMLEMaximum Likelihood Estimate obtained from the expert demonstrations D

P(T(s',a)|D) = Dir(Ns,a+ 1|s,a)Probabilistic model over T without making any optimality assumption. Where 1 is a vector of 1's of dimension |S| and Ns,ais the number of transitions from state s and action a in the batch data D

sections.title: Constraints
content: Using the reward R and near-optimal expert policy πewe create a set of constraints with respect to the dynamics T to impose the values of the actions of the expert to be superior to the ones of unseen actions.

sections.title: Method
content: Sample from P(T|D) and only keep samples that satisfies the constraints.
Create empirical posterior P(T|D, πe) with accepted samples. This will be the estimate of the posterior on T given that the demonstrations from the expert are near-optimal

sections.title: Results
content: • MLE
T̂ = TMLE—Value Iteration→ (πMLE, QMLE) = (π̂, Q̂) —Compute Results→
• P(T|D, πe) and P(T|D)
{T̂(i)}i=11000~ Posterior —Value Iteration→ {(π̂(i), Q̂(i))}i=11000—Empirical Mean→ {(π̂, Q̂)} —Compute Results→
P(T|D, πe) outperforms baselines by being more accurate and avoiding low value actions
P(T|D, πe) exhibits significantly less variance over different datasets
P(T|D, πe) allows quantifying uncertainty on corresponding policies

figures.description: Grid of 6 plots comparing Q_metric for MLE, P(T|D), and P(T|D, pi_e) across different epsilon (0, 3, 4) and data levels (Low vs High)
